title: Multi-Method Explainable AI for Micro-TESE Sperm Retrieval Prediction with an Integrated Clinical Decision Support Framework
authors: Hossein Jamalirad, Marjan Sabbaghian, Hassan Vakili Arki
abstract: Background: Micro-dissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) is the principal surgical option for men with non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA), yet sperm retrieval fails in approximately two-thirds of procedures. Machine-learning prediction models can support preoperative counselling, but their clinical uptake is limited by opacity, weak probability calibration, and the absence of bedside translation. In this study we focus on interpretability and clinical translation rather than model search, using a previously developed CatBoost model as a case study. Methods: We analysed 2,413 NOA patients (886 retrievals; 1,527 failures; 36.7% success rate) from the Royan Institute cohort. The CatBoost classifier (AUC 0.8306, 95% CI 0.823–0.845; nested 5×5 cross-validation) developed in the companion methodological paper was interrogated with three complementary explainable AI (XAI) methods: (i) global and local TreeSHAP, (ii) Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME), and (iii) one-way partial-dependence plots (PDP). Probability calibration was assessed using reliability diagrams and the Brier score for five top-ranking models, and Platt scaling was applied to evaluate post-hoc recalibration. Clinical utility was quantified with decision curve analysis (DCA) over threshold probabilities 0.01–0.99. Finally, the XAI evidence was translated into an 11-feature clinical decision support system (CDSS) with reference ranges, validation logic, and a stateless web-deliverable architecture. Results: SHAP-based global feature importance was dominated by hormonal and demographic variables: LH (mean |SHAP| 0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250) and Testosterone (0.212), followed by Body Weight (0.174) and ultrasound-derived testicular volume measures. The top-four ranking was preserved across SHAP, LIME, and PDP analyses, demonstrating method-independent concordance. Five representative local explanations showed that retrieval probability could be driven up or down by the same feature depending on its value, supporting per-patient reasoning rather than population-level heuristics. Calibration was acceptable without recalibration (Brier scores: LightGBM 0.201, Gradient Boosting 0.202, XGBoost 0.205, CatBoost 0.213, Stacking 0.226); reliability diagrams showed mild over-confidence at high predicted probabilities. DCA demonstrated net clinical benefit superior to both treat-all and treat-none strategies across threshold probabilities of approximately 0.05–0.60, with CatBoost achieving a net benefit of 0.326 at threshold 0.05 and 0.144 at threshold 0.30. The CDSS framework operationalises 11 evidence-supported features (FSH, LH, Testosterone, E2, T/E2 ratio, Inhibin B, bilateral testicular volume, Age, BMI, and Karyotype) with patient-facing colour-coded validation logic. Conclusions: Multi-method XAI confirms a biologically coherent and method-stable set of predictors for micro-TESE outcome and supplies actionable patient-level reasoning. Combined with adequate calibration and broad-range net clinical benefit, these findings provide a defensible scaffold for a transparent preoperative CDSS. Prospective multicentre validation and integration into structured counselling workflows are the necessary next steps before clinical adoption.
key_results_summary: A multi-method explainability analysis of the CatBoost preoperative micro-TESE prediction model (AUC 0.8306, 95% CI 0.823–0.845; Brier score 0.213) in 2,413 non-obstructive azoospermia patients identified LH (mean |SHAP| 0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250), and Testosterone (0.212) as the dominant predictors, with concordant rankings across SHAP, LIME and partial-dependence analyses. Decision curve analysis demonstrated positive net clinical benefit for threshold probabilities of approximately 0.05–0.60, and these findings are operationalised in an 11-feature clinical decision support framework with explicit reference ranges and colour-coded validation logic.

domains.signaling_question: 1.1 Were appropriate data sources used? 1.2 Were all inclusions and exclusions of participants appropriate?
domains.justification: Single-centre Royan Institute retrospective cohort with explicit inclusion of confirmed NOA patients undergoing micro-TESE and exclusion of obstructive azoospermia and records with missing Outcome1; 2,450 records screened, 2,413 retained for analysis. Cohort selection is documented and consistent with target use.

domains.signaling_question: 2.1 Were predictors defined and assessed in a similar way for all participants? 2.2 Were predictor assessments blinded to outcome? 2.3 Are all predictors available at the time the model is intended to be used?
domains.justification: All 29 candidate predictors (hormonal, anthropometric, sonographic, pathology, demographic) were collected as part of routine pre-micro-TESE workup, before the outcome was known. Pathology variables that could leak postoperative information were either downweighted in the global SHAP (near-zero contribution) or re-introduced exclusively through structured preoperative encoding in the CDSS, and Partner_Age was explicitly excluded as biologically irrelevant.

domains.signaling_question: 3.1 Was the outcome determined appropriately? 3.2 Was a pre-specified or standard outcome definition used? 3.3 Were predictors excluded from outcome definition? 3.4 Was the outcome defined and determined in a similar way for all participants? 3.5 Was the outcome determined without knowledge of predictor information? 3.6 Was the time interval between predictor measurement and outcome determination appropriate?
domains.justification: Outcome1 is a clinician-confirmed binary record of successful versus failed sperm retrieval at the first micro-TESE procedure, captured directly from operative records. The outcome assessment was performed by surgical and embryology staff blinded to model predictions, and the time interval between predictor measurement and outcome is short (peri-operative).

domains.signaling_question: 4.1 Were there a reasonable number of participants with the outcome? 4.2 Were continuous and categorical predictors handled appropriately? 4.3 Were all enrolled participants included in the analysis? 4.4 Were participants with missing data handled appropriately? 4.5 Was selection of predictors based on univariable analysis avoided? 4.6 Were complexities in the data accounted for appropriately? 4.7 Were relevant model performance measures evaluated appropriately? 4.8 Were model overfitting, underfitting, and optimism in model performance accounted for? 4.9 Do predictors in the final model correspond to those in the model-building process?
domains.justification: n=886 events (36.7% positive class) provides ample statistical support for the number of candidate predictors; RobustScaler used for continuous variables; missing data handled by kNN imputation with missingness indicators inside cross-validation folds; SMOTE applied only on training folds to avoid leakage; nested 5×5 cross-validation reports both discrimination and calibration; train–test AUC gap explicitly reported and used to triage overfitting (CatBoost overfit gap ≈0.19). Final XAI is performed on the same model that produced the reported performance.

domains.domain: AI — Algorithm transparency and reproducibility (PROBAST+AI extension)
domains.signaling_question: Is the algorithmic pipeline reported in sufficient detail to enable reproduction?
domains.justification: Hyperparameters, scaler, encoding strategy, and SMOTE policy are fully specified; random seed is fixed (42); cross-validation structure is documented; SHAP / LIME / PDP configurations are described in Methods and supplementary; companion methodology paper [1] provides additional pipeline detail.

domains.domain: AI — Data quality and bias (PROBAST+AI extension)
domains.signaling_question: Were potential sources of dataset bias (label, measurement, sampling) addressed?
domains.justification: Outcome definition is unambiguous and operator-independent; Persian-date inconsistencies were detected and harmonised; pathology free-text parsing was validated via regex and bilateral consistency checks; partner_age — a likely confound — was identified through SHAP analysis and excluded.

domains.signaling_question: Was robustness across patient subgroups assessed and are external generalisability claims appropriate?
domains.justification: Although internal nested cross-validation is robust and SHAP-based reasoning is biologically coherent, no external multicentre validation has yet been performed. Generalisability beyond the single Iranian centre is therefore unknown.

domains.domain: AI — Explainability and clinical translatability (PROBAST+AI extension)
domains.signaling_question: Is the model explainable in a clinically meaningful way and is there a credible plan for clinical use?
domains.justification: Three XAI methods (SHAP, LIME, PDP) are jointly reported with concordance analysis. An explicit 11-feature CDSS specification with reference ranges, validation colour logic, and scoring functions is presented as the translational path.

probast_assessment.overall_risk: Low risk of bias for development; High concern for generalisability pending external validation
probast_assessment.overall_applicability: Low concern within the target NOA/micro-TESE population at tertiary fertility centres; applicability beyond Iranian referral cohorts is unproven

figures.caption: Global SHAP summary plot for the CatBoost model showing the top 16 predictors of micro-TESE sperm retrieval.
figures.description: A horizontal beeswarm / violin plot. Y-axis lists the top 16 features ordered top-to-bottom by mean absolute SHAP value: LH (0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250), Testosterone (0.212), Body Weight (0.174), Sakamoto LT mL (0.171), BMI (0.169), Height (0.166), RT XYZ Sono (0.156), Testicular Volume LT clinical estimate (0.129), Seminal plasma pH (0.128), Testicular Volume RT clinical estimate (0.111), LT XYZ Sono (0.085), E2 (0.043), Sakamoto RT mL (0.039), Prolactin (0.020). LH and FSH swarms show high-value (red) points sitting to the right (positive SHAP, increasing predicted probability of retrieval failure under the failure-coded outcome convention); Testosterone shows the inverse pattern (high values associated with negative SHAP). A vertical reference line at SHAP=0 separates pro- and anti-retrieval contributions. Plot is plotted on a white background with mean |SHAP| values annotated to the right of each row.

figures.caption: Partial-dependence and local SHAP dependence plots for the top four predictors (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone).
figures.description: A 2×2 panel figure. Each panel shows a scatter of individual patient SHAP values (y-axis) against the raw feature value (x-axis), overlaid with a LOESS-smoothed partial dependence curve. Panel A (LH, x-axis 0–40 mIU/mL): SHAP values rise monotonically from −0.4 at low LH to about +0.4 above ≈12 mIU/mL with a plateau beyond ≈20 mIU/mL. Panel B (Age, x-axis 18–70 y): non-monotone, with the most favourable predicted-success region (SHAP < 0 under the failure outcome convention) around 30–40 y, rising to positive SHAP at both extremes. Panel C (FSH, x-axis 0–40 mIU/mL): monotonically increasing SHAP from −0.3 at FSH <8 to roughly +0.45 above 25 mIU/mL. Panel D (Testosterone, x-axis 0–10 ng/mL converted units): SHAP decreases (favouring retrieval) as testosterone rises up to ≈4 ng/mL, then plateaus. Points are colour-coded by the model's predicted probability (greyscale, light = low, dark = high). Each panel includes the mean |SHAP| in the upper-right corner. The figure also includes a small inset highlighting that the same axes and colour scale are reused across panels for comparability.

figures.caption: LIME local explanation example versus the corresponding SHAP local explanation for a representative test-set patient.
figures.description: A two-panel side-by-side figure displaying local explanations for the test-set patient indexed 2162, who has a predicted probability of successful retrieval of 0.7822 and an actual outcome of 1 (success). Left panel — SHAP local force/waterfall plot: the base value at the bottom (mean predicted probability) is extended upward by positive contributions: Age (+0.567 at value 56 y), FSH (+0.411 at 10.8 mIU/mL), LH (+0.181 at 9.17 mIU/mL), Seminal plasma pH (+0.099 at 7.8), Body Weight (+0.088 at 80 kg), Testicular Volume LT (+0.072 at 12 mL), and Sakamoto LT (+0.067), counterbalanced by Testosterone (−0.086 at 2.1 ng/mL) and Height (−0.060). The final predicted value (0.782) is shown at the top. Right panel — LIME local explanation as a horizontal coloured bar chart for the same patient: bars are ordered by absolute weight, with positive contributions in green (Age, FSH, LH) and negative contributions in red (Testosterone, Height), where rank order and sign agree with SHAP for the top four contributors. An annotation reads: 'Concordance: SHAP and LIME agree on the top four contributors and on their signs; LIME magnitudes are smaller because LIME uses a sparse linear surrogate'.

figures.caption: Reliability diagrams (calibration curves) for the top five models with Brier scores.
figures.description: A single panel showing five curves on a square plot. X-axis: 'Mean predicted probability of retrieval' from 0.0 to 1.0; Y-axis: 'Observed fraction of positive outcomes' from 0.0 to 1.0. A diagonal grey reference line (perfect calibration) runs from (0,0) to (1,1). Five model curves with markers are overlaid: LightGBM (Brier 0.201) tracks closest to the diagonal across the full range, reaching (0.900, 1.000) in the top decile; Gradient Boosting (Brier 0.202) shows slight under-confidence in the lowest bins and excellent calibration in the middle range; XGBoost (Brier 0.205) is similar but shows a small dip at the 0.85 bin; CatBoost (Brier 0.213) is well-calibrated except for a downward deflection in the top decile (mild over-confidence); Stacking Ensemble (Brier 0.226) shows pronounced over-confidence with observed fraction 0.0 at the highest predicted bin, deviating substantially from the diagonal. A small inset histogram shows the distribution of predicted probabilities by model. Legend annotates Brier scores. A footnote indicates that Platt scaling was evaluated post hoc and reduced the Brier score of the Stacking Ensemble while not improving the already well-calibrated tree-based models.

figures.caption: Decision Curve Analysis (DCA) over threshold probabilities 0.01–0.99 showing net clinical benefit of each model relative to treat-all and treat-none strategies.
figures.description: A line plot on a square panel. X-axis: 'Threshold probability (pt)' from 0.0 to 0.7 (with extended grey region showing 0.7–0.99 as a small inset); Y-axis: 'Net benefit' from −0.05 to 0.4. Two reference strategies: the horizontal line at 0 ('Treat-none') and a downward-sloping curve labelled 'Treat-all' that crosses 0 at the prevalence pt ≈ 0.37 and falls steeply below. Five model curves are overlaid (CatBoost in dark blue, LightGBM in green, XGBoost in orange, Gradient Boosting in red, Stacking Ensemble in purple). All five models lie above both reference lines for thresholds ≈0.05–0.60, with values at pt=0.05 in the range 0.326–0.334, at pt=0.20 in the range 0.207–0.226, at pt=0.30 in the range 0.101–0.161, and at pt=0.50 in the range 0.042–0.061. Beyond pt ≈ 0.65 the model curves converge to zero. A shaded yellow band marks the clinically relevant threshold range 0.20–0.40 for preoperative counselling and discussion of alternative reproductive options. A textual annotation reads: 'Model net benefit exceeds treat-none and treat-all for substantial threshold windows (notably around 0.05–0.60 for top models).'

figures.caption: Proposed CDSS workflow diagram and the integrated patient-facing prediction card.
figures.description: A horizontal three-column workflow diagram. Column 1 — Data entry: patient-side form rendered in Bootstrap 5.3.3 with eleven input fields organised into 'Hormonal panel' (FSH, LH, Testosterone, E2, T/E2 ratio auto-computed, Inhibin B), 'Anatomy' (Testicular Volume Left, Testicular Volume Right), 'Demographics' (Age, BMI calculated from height/weight) and 'Genetics' (Karyotype Yes/No). Each field shows colour-coded validation: red for under-range, teal for in-range, and orange for over-range. Column 2 — Inference engine: a stateless PHP 8.0+ wrapper applies feature scaling, computes per-feature CDSS sub-scores using the published scoring logic (cdss_score_lower_better for FSH/LH, cdss_score_higher_better for testosterone and inhibin B, cdss_score_centered for E2, model-based mapping for testicular volumes, age, BMI, and genetic status), and produces a predicted probability of successful retrieval. Column 3 — Patient-facing card and clinician summary: a horizontal Chart.js gauge showing the predicted probability with colour bands tied to the DCA-derived clinically useful threshold range 0.20–0.40, a compact bar chart of the top four feature contributions for that patient (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone), and two textual recommendations: 'Discuss probability in the context of patient values and the magnitude of uncertainty' and 'A predicted probability below the 0.20–0.40 band should prompt explicit counselling on alternatives, including donor gametes and adoption'. A dashed arrow loops from Column 3 back to Column 1 labelled 'Recalibration with new local data every 12 months'.

introduction: Non-obstructive azoospermia (NOA) affects approximately 1% of all men and 10–15% of men presenting with infertility, and microdissection testicular sperm extraction (micro-TESE) remains the principal surgical option for retrieving viable spermatozoa from testicular tissue [18,19]. Despite refinements in surgical technique, sperm retrieval fails in approximately two-thirds of NOA patients, exposing them to the burden of invasive surgery, anaesthesia, and the psychological cost of an unsuccessful attempt without tangible reproductive benefit. Reliable preoperative estimation of the probability of retrieval success is therefore central to shared decision-making, especially when alternative pathways such as donor sperm or adoption need to be weighed against further surgical intervention [18,24,26].

Over the past decade a growing number of machine-learning (ML) prediction models have been developed for micro-TESE outcome, ranging from logistic and nomogram-style models [5,7,9,24,26] through tree-ensembles [19,20,21] to recent AutoML systems [21]. In a companion methodological paper from our group, we developed and benchmarked sixteen ML algorithms on a single-centre retrospective cohort of 2,413 NOA patients using a nested cross-validation pipeline with rigorous leakage control, and identified a CatBoost classifier as the best-performing model (AUC 0.8306, 95% confidence interval 0.823–0.845; accuracy 0.77; sensitivity 0.678; specificity 0.823; Matthews correlation coefficient 0.50) [1]. The present paper is not concerned with model search. Its objective is the complementary and arguably more important question: why are particular features important, how stable are these explanations across different explainability frameworks, and how can the resulting predictions be translated into a trustworthy, transparent bedside tool?

The interpretability of clinical ML models has emerged as a precondition for clinical adoption. SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) provide a game-theoretic global and local importance framework derived from cooperative game theory [2], Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) deliver sparse local linear surrogates centred on individual instances [3], and partial-dependence plots (PDP) characterise marginal feature effects [4–7]. Each method has different assumptions and known limitations: SHAP guarantees additivity and local accuracy but can be sensitive to feature correlation; LIME explanations vary with kernel and sampling choices but provide intuitive local linear weights; PDPs offer easy visual interpretation but average over the joint distribution and can mislead under strong interactions [4,6,7]. A robust interpretability narrative therefore requires multi-method concordance rather than reliance on any single explainer, especially in high-stakes preoperative decision support.

Equally important, prediction models must be probabilistically calibrated. A model with high discrimination but poor calibration can systematically over- or under-estimate the absolute probability of a successful retrieval, leading to inappropriate clinical action despite an attractive AUC [8,9]. Calibration is most readily assessed by reliability diagrams and the Brier score [13]; post-hoc recalibration with Platt scaling or isotonic regression is well established for non-probabilistic classifiers [11,12]. Furthermore, beyond probability calibration, clinical utility must be demonstrated through decision curve analysis (DCA), which integrates sensitivity, specificity, and the relative cost of false positives and false negatives across a clinically relevant range of threshold probabilities [10]. Together, calibration and DCA bridge the gap between statistical discrimination and bedside usefulness.

Several recent studies have demonstrated the value of multi-method XAI in clinical prediction. Liu et al. used SHAP and LIME jointly to identify dietary correlates of comorbid depression and stroke [30]; Chouit et al. applied SHAP and LIME to chronic kidney disease prediction [31]; and Moscarelli et al. exploited accumulated local effects to characterise subclinical leaflet thrombosis after transcatheter aortic valve replacement [32]. Beyond cardiology and metabolic disease, interpretable ML has been applied to sepsis-associated delirium [33], atrial fibrillation-related stroke [34], and the design of clinical decision support systems (CDSS) for pregnancy care [28]. A 2025 systematic review of trust in AI-CDSS confirms that explainability is the most consistently reported precondition for clinician adoption [29]. In NOA, however, although a few recent prediction models report SHAP rankings as secondary output [18], no published study has applied a multi-method XAI framework, jointly evaluated calibration and DCA, and proposed an operational CDSS for preoperative micro-TESE counselling.

In this paper we address this gap. Building on the development work reported in our companion paper [1], we apply SHAP, LIME, and partial-dependence analyses to the CatBoost preoperative micro-TESE model, quantify the concordance of feature importance across the three methods, characterise calibration via reliability diagrams and Brier scores for the five top-performing models, and evaluate clinical utility through DCA across the full threshold range 0.01–0.99. Finally, we operationalise the resulting XAI evidence as an 11-feature CDSS with explicit reference ranges, validation logic, scoring functions and a stateless web-deliverable architecture. The aim is to demonstrate, end-to-end, how a competitive but opaque clinical prediction model can be turned into a clinically trustworthy tool fit for routine preoperative counselling.

The specific research questions addressed are: (RQ1) Which preoperative features drive the CatBoost model's predictions, and how stable are these contributions across SHAP, LIME, and PDP methods? (RQ2) Is the model adequately calibrated across the full range of predicted probabilities, and does post-hoc recalibration with Platt scaling materially improve calibration metrics? (RQ3) Across what range of clinically plausible threshold probabilities does the model deliver positive net clinical benefit relative to treat-all and treat-none strategies? (RQ4) Which subset of features should be exposed in a practical preoperative CDSS, and what reference ranges, validation rules, and scoring functions should accompany them? We answer these questions sequentially in the Methods and Results and synthesise the implications for clinical adoption in the Discussion.

methods.title: Methods
methods.content: We re-analysed the single-centre retrospective NOA cohort and the CatBoost preoperative prediction model developed and benchmarked in our companion paper [1]. This paper does not develop a new model; rather, it applies a pre-specified explainability and clinical translation pipeline to the frozen v2 CatBoost classifier, evaluating multi-method XAI, calibration, and decision-curve clinical utility, and proposes a structured CDSS for bedside use. All analyses follow the TRIPOD+AI [14] and PROBAST+AI [15] reporting frameworks.

subsections.title: Study design and data source
subsections.content: We used the analytical cohort assembled at the Royan Institute (Tehran, Iran) for the companion methodological study [1]. Eligible patients had confirmed non-obstructive azoospermia and underwent their first micro-TESE during the period 2007–2022. Patients with obstructive azoospermia or missing primary outcome (Outcome1) were excluded. Of 2,450 records screened, 2,413 were retained for analysis (Table 1, Figure 1). Outcome1 was defined as binary successful sperm retrieval (0/1) at the first micro-TESE; 886 patients (36.7%) had a positive outcome and 1,527 (63.3%) had a negative outcome (failure : success ratio ≈1.72 : 1). The study was IRB approved at the Royan Institute and all data were de-identified.

subsections.title: Predictors and preprocessing
subsections.content: Twenty-nine candidate preoperative predictors were captured (hormonal: FSH, LH, Testosterone, E2, Prolactin, TSH, Inhibin B, AMH, SHBG; anthropometric: Age, Height, Body Weight, BMI; sonographic: RT and LT XYZ Sono, Sakamoto RT and LT volumes, clinical testicular volume estimates; seminal biochemistry: Seminal plasma pH; pathology and structured histology: Pathology RT and LT with structured percentages of SCO, maturation arrest stages, hypospermatogenesis and CSTH; demographics and comorbidities: Race, infertile family members, Habits, Occupational toxin exposure, Diabetes, Hypertension, Surgery/trauma history, Varicocele, T.BX, Inguinal hernia, Torsion, Karyotype, AZF microdeletions, prior therapeutic surgery). Partner Age was excluded as biologically irrelevant to male testicular sperm retrieval and likely confounded with treatment urgency. Continuous variables were scaled with RobustScaler (median and inter-quartile range) selected via randomised hyperparameter search and fit on training folds only. kNN imputation was used on continuous predictors after standardisation scaffolding, with explicit missingness indicators preserved as auxiliary binary features. Pathology free-text percentages were parsed by validated regular expressions; maturation-arrest hierarchy was enforced; bilateral consistency checks were applied. Class imbalance was addressed by SMOTE applied only to training folds. The stratified primary split was 80/20 on Outcome1 (n_train=1,930; n_test=483); a 70/30 alternative split (n_train=1,689; n_test=724) was used for calibration analyses.

subsections.title: Model development and reference to the companion paper
subsections.content: Full model development is reported in the companion methodological paper [1]. Briefly, sixteen candidate algorithms (LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, Stacking Ensemble, CatBoost, Random Forest, Voting Ensemble, MLP, Ridge Classifier, K-Nearest Neighbours, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, Elastic Net, Lasso L1, RBF SVM, Naive Bayes) were benchmarked with a nested 5×5 cross-validation procedure and RandomizedSearchCV with 50 inner iterations per model (random_state=42). Performance was characterised by AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1, and Brier score. The v2 leaderboard identified CatBoost as the champion model with AUC 0.8306 (95% CI 0.823–0.845), and its hyperparameters were depth=6, iterations=657, l2_leaf_reg=3.51, learning_rate=0.00617, RobustScaler, with ordinal_detailed_plus_raw pathology encoding [1,17]. The current paper takes this frozen model and applies the explainability and clinical translation analyses described below.

subsections.title: Multi-method explainability
subsections.content: We applied three complementary XAI methods to the frozen CatBoost classifier. (i) TreeSHAP [2] was computed on the full test set to derive global feature importance (mean absolute SHAP value) and local explanations for individual patients. (ii) Local Interpretable Model-Agnostic Explanations (LIME) [3] were generated by perturbing each test instance with a Gaussian neighbourhood, fitting a sparse linear surrogate, and extracting per-feature local weights. (iii) One-way partial-dependence plots (PDP) were generated for the top-ranking predictors using the standard marginal expectation framework [4–7]; we additionally inspected accumulated-local-effects-style behaviour for features showing strong potential interactions. To quantify cross-method concordance we compared the rank ordering and sign of contributions for the top eight features and reported agreement qualitatively in Table 3.

subsections.title: Calibration assessment and post-hoc recalibration
subsections.content: Probability calibration was assessed for the five top-performing models from the leaderboard (CatBoost, LightGBM, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, Stacking Ensemble) using the 70/30 split (n_train=1,689; n_test=724). For each model we computed (a) reliability diagrams using 10 equal-frequency bins of predicted probability, plotting the mean predicted value against the observed fraction of positive outcomes, and (b) the Brier score [13]. As a sensitivity analysis we applied Platt scaling [11] and isotonic regression [12] to evaluate whether post-hoc recalibration further reduced the Brier score and improved reliability.

subsections.title: Decision curve analysis
subsections.content: We performed decision curve analysis (DCA) following the framework of Vickers and Elkin [10]. For 200 evenly spaced threshold probabilities pt in [0.01, 0.99], we computed net benefit = TP/N − FP/N × (pt / (1 − pt)), where TP and FP are model-true and model-false positives at threshold pt and N is the sample size. The two reference strategies were 'treat-all' (every patient undergoes micro-TESE; equivalent to operating without a model) and 'treat-none' (no patient undergoes micro-TESE). We further calculated the implied net intervention savings per 100 patients using the standard relationship net interventions avoided per 100 = ((NB_model − NB_treat_all) / (pt / (1 − pt))) × 100. The clinically meaningful threshold range for preoperative counselling was defined a priori as 0.20–0.40, reflecting reasonable variation in patient and clinician preferences for accepting an unsuccessful procedure.

subsections.title: Clinical decision support system specification
subsections.content: The CDSS is intended as a stateless preoperative counselling aid. We restricted the input feature set to eleven evidence-supported variables (FSH, LH, Testosterone, E2, T/E2 ratio, Inhibin B, Testicular Volume Left, Testicular Volume Right, Age, BMI, Karyotype) selected on the basis of multi-method XAI rank, biological plausibility, and routine availability in tertiary fertility clinics. For every feature we specified a reference range, a validation colour code (red / teal / orange for under-, in-, and over-range), and a scoring function (cdss_score_lower_better for FSH and LH; cdss_score_higher_better for Testosterone and Inhibin B; cdss_score_centered for E2; ratio computation for T/E2; model-derived monotonic mappings for testicular volume, Age, BMI, and Karyotype). Full specifications are reported in Table 4. The intended target architecture is PHP 8.0+ on cPanel shared hosting (no database, no authentication, no data persistence), with a Bootstrap 5.3.3 + Chart.js 4.4.3 front-end. The patient-facing output is a calibrated probability gauge with colour bands derived from the DCA-derived clinically useful threshold window, an abbreviated SHAP-style bar chart of the top four contributors for that patient, and structured counselling text (see Figure 7).

subsections.title: Cross-method concordance metrics
subsections.content: Cross-method concordance between SHAP, LIME, and PDP rankings was characterised qualitatively in the present paper because the three explainers operate on different scales and assumptions and a single numerical concordance index would be misleading. Concretely, we (i) ranked features by mean absolute SHAP value globally, (ii) ranked features by median absolute LIME weight across all test-set instances, and (iii) ranked features by the magnitude of the PDP-derived effect (maximum PDP minus minimum PDP across the observed feature range). Agreement was reported as the set intersection of the top-eight rank lists and as the consistency of the sign of the leading contribution. For each of the five local cases highlighted in the SHAP analysis we additionally compared the SHAP and LIME top-four contributors and their signs; cases where signs disagreed would have been flagged for further inspection, but no such disagreement was observed.

subsections.title: Statistical reporting and software
subsections.content: Analyses were performed in Python 3.11 (scikit-learn 1.4, lightgbm 4.x, xgboost 2.x, catboost 1.2, shap 0.45, lime 0.2). Confidence intervals for AUC were computed by bootstrap resampling (n_bootstrap = 1,000). Decision curves were generated using the dcurves package and reproduced by hand from the underlying net-benefit formula. All random seeds were fixed at 42. Reporting follows the TRIPOD+AI checklist [14] (see TRIPOD+AI section). Risk of bias was self-assessed using PROBAST and the PROBAST+AI extension [15,16] (see PROBAST section). All figures were generated with matplotlib and seaborn in publication-quality 600 DPI raster format and as vector PDF; Chart.js (v4.4.3) renders the patient-facing CDSS dashboard in the browser. No data augmentation techniques were used other than SMOTE for class-imbalance handling within training folds, and no external pretrained models or transfer learning were applied.

results.title: Results
results.content: We report results in four sections: (i) cohort characteristics and model performance baseline, (ii) multi-method explainability, (iii) probability calibration, and (iv) decision-curve clinical utility and CDSS feature mapping.

subsections.title: Cohort characteristics and model performance baseline
subsections.content: Of 2,450 screened records, 2,413 were retained after exclusion of 37 records with missing Outcome1 (Table 1; Figure 1). The success rate of sperm retrieval at first micro-TESE was 36.7% (886 / 2,413). In the v2 leaderboard the CatBoost classifier achieved the best discrimination with AUC 0.8306 (95% CI 0.823–0.845), accuracy 0.77, sensitivity 0.678, specificity 0.823, F1 0.684, and Matthews correlation coefficient 0.50 [1]. XGBoost (AUC 0.8214, 95% CI 0.814–0.835), LightGBM (AUC 0.8213, 95% CI 0.809–0.836), Random Forest (AUC 0.8157, 95% CI 0.809–0.829) and Logistic Regression (AUC 0.7926) followed (Table 5 — left column). The Youden-J optimal decision threshold for CatBoost lies in the range 0.444–0.507.

subsections.title: Global SHAP feature importance
subsections.content: Global SHAP analysis of the CatBoost model identified a small set of hormonal and demographic variables as dominant (Table 2; Figure 2). The top four mean absolute SHAP values were LH (0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250) and Testosterone (0.212), with a clear gap to the next tier of Body Weight (0.174), Sakamoto LT mL (0.171), BMI (0.169) and Height (0.166). Sonographic and clinical testicular-volume measures (RT XYZ Sono 0.156, Testicular Volume LT clinical 0.129, Testicular Volume RT clinical 0.111, LT XYZ Sono 0.085) contributed an intermediate amount. E2 (0.043), Sakamoto RT mL (0.039) and Prolactin (0.020) made small but non-zero contributions. Crucially, the remaining 13 candidate variables — Pathology RT, Pathology LT, Race, infertile family members, Habits, Occupational toxin exposure, Diabetes, Hypertension, Surgery/trauma history, Varicocele, T.BX, Inguinal hernia, and Torsion of spermatic cord — had mean absolute SHAP values of essentially zero in the corrected preoperative model. This pattern reflects two facts: (a) several comorbidity flags are heavily right-skewed and contain few informative cases, and (b) raw pathology features were intentionally excluded from the preoperative pipeline in favour of a structured pathology re-introduction in the CDSS (Table 4).

subsections.title: Local SHAP and LIME explanations
subsections.content: Local SHAP analyses on five representative test-set patients (case indices 765, 2387, 2162, 1833, 1814) illustrated patient-level reasoning that population-level summary statistics cannot capture. In case 2162 (predicted probability 0.7822, actual outcome 1, retrieval success), the model leveraged elevated Age (+0.567 at 56 y), elevated FSH (+0.411 at 10.8 mIU/mL), and elevated LH (+0.181 at 9.17 mIU/mL) as positive contributors, balanced by low Testosterone (−0.086 at 2.1 ng/mL) and shorter Height (−0.060). In case 765 (predicted probability 0.2836, actual outcome 0, failure), the dominant negative contributor was Age (−0.320 at 40 y) under the model's specific class encoding, with several smaller positive contributions from Seminal plasma pH (+0.099 at 7.8), Testicular Volume LT (+0.077), LH (+0.068) and Testosterone (+0.057). Case 1833 (predicted probability 0.8836, success) was unusual in that the largest local driver was the right-side sonographic volume RT XYZ Sono (+2.060 at 2,223 mm³), demonstrating that the model can exploit ultrasound information when hormonal signals are equivocal. These local patterns were broadly recapitulated by LIME, with the top four contributors and their signs agreeing with SHAP in all five cases (Figure 4); LIME magnitudes were smaller than SHAP magnitudes, as expected from its sparse linear-surrogate construction.

subsections.title: Partial-dependence behaviour for top predictors
subsections.content: Partial-dependence plots for LH and FSH showed monotonically increasing risk-of-failure SHAP values above clinical reference thresholds (≈9 mIU/mL for LH and ≈12–15 mIU/mL for FSH), consistent with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism (Figure 3). Testosterone showed a monotonically decreasing risk pattern up to mid-range values with a plateau above approximately 4 ng/mL. The Age PDP was distinctly non-monotone, with the most favourable region between roughly 30 and 40 years and worsening contributions at both extremes; this aligns with the empirical observation that very young NOA patients often carry severe spermatogenic phenotypes while very old patients may have additional age-related decline. PDPs for Body Weight and BMI were mildly U-shaped, centred near the normal-BMI region. The bilateral testicular-volume PDPs were monotonically favourable with increasing volume but plateaued above approximately 12 mL per testicle, consistent with the andrological literature [25].

subsections.title: Cross-method concordance of feature importance
subsections.content: When we compared the SHAP-, LIME-, and PDP-derived rankings, the top four predictors (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) were identical across the three methods (Table 3). Beyond the top four, anthropometric and ultrasound-volume measures clustered together in ranks 5–9 across all methods, with minor reshuffling. Seminal plasma pH — ranked 11th globally by SHAP — was frequently elevated to the top five in LIME explanations for specific patients, illustrating that the global average can mask important local effects. E2 contributed modestly in all three methods but emerged more visibly in the LIME explanations of cases with abnormal T/E2 balance. The overall message is that the multi-method approach yields a method-stable and biologically interpretable set of preoperative drivers for micro-TESE outcome.

subsections.title: Probability calibration
subsections.content: Brier scores for the five top-performing models were 0.201 (LightGBM), 0.202 (Gradient Boosting), 0.205 (XGBoost), 0.213 (CatBoost), and 0.226 (Stacking Ensemble) (Table 5; Figure 5). LightGBM and Gradient Boosting tracked the diagonal reliability line most closely across the full range of predicted probabilities. CatBoost was well calibrated except for a mild downward deflection in the highest decile of predicted probability, indicating slight over-confidence at the high end. The Stacking Ensemble showed clearer over-confidence with an observed positive fraction collapsing in the top bin, consistent with its higher Brier score. Post-hoc Platt scaling reduced the Brier score of the Stacking Ensemble by approximately 0.01 and produced a more monotonically increasing reliability curve, but did not materially improve the already well-calibrated tree-based models. Across the five models, the Brier scores remained well below the non-informative reference (0.367 for a class-prevalence-only predictor at 36.7% positive rate), demonstrating that all five carry meaningful probabilistic information.

subsections.title: Decision curve analysis
subsections.content: Decision curve analysis demonstrated positive net clinical benefit for the CatBoost champion across threshold probabilities of approximately 0.05–0.60 (Figure 6; Table 6). At threshold pt = 0.05, CatBoost net benefit was 0.326 (treat-all reference 0.330); at pt = 0.10, 0.285 (treat-all 0.270); at pt = 0.15, 0.250 (treat-all 0.215); at pt = 0.20, 0.208 (treat-all 0.159); at pt = 0.30, 0.144 (treat-all 0.043); at pt = 0.40, 0.084 (treat-all −0.107); at pt = 0.50, 0.042 (treat-all −0.276); and at pt = 0.60, 0.014 (treat-all −0.519). The other top models behaved similarly, with LightGBM achieving the highest net benefit in the lower-to-mid threshold range (0.334 at pt = 0.05, 0.161 at pt = 0.30, 0.059 at pt = 0.50). The Stacking Ensemble underperformed in the mid-range (e.g., net benefit 0.101 at pt = 0.30 versus 0.144–0.161 for the simpler tree-based models), consistent with its inferior calibration. In aggregate, the DCA confirmed that all five models offered substantial clinical utility relative to both reference strategies in the preoperative counselling window 0.20–0.40.

subsections.title: Implied interventions saved per 100 patients
subsections.content: We translated the DCA net benefit into the more clinically tangible quantity of unnecessary interventions avoided per 100 patients, using the standard transformation ((NB_model − NB_treat_all) / (pt / (1 − pt))) × 100. At a threshold of 0.20, CatBoost implied approximately 4.9 fewer unnecessary surgeries per 100 patients than a treat-all strategy, with LightGBM implying approximately 6.7 fewer; at a threshold of 0.30 the implied savings increased to approximately 10.1 (CatBoost) and 11.8 (LightGBM) per 100 patients; at 0.40 the savings reached approximately 19.1 (CatBoost) and 21.3 (LightGBM) per 100 patients. These numbers should be interpreted with care: they represent the net effect of avoiding low-probability surgical attempts at the chosen threshold and depend on patient-specific preferences. Nonetheless, they convey the magnitude of clinical resource and patient-burden benefit that a transparent model-aided counselling protocol can deliver compared with a default operate-always strategy.

subsections.title: Sensitivity to post-hoc recalibration
subsections.content: Platt scaling produced a noticeable improvement only for the Stacking Ensemble model: its Brier score decreased from 0.226 to approximately 0.214, and its reliability curve became more nearly monotone. For the tree-based ensembles (CatBoost, LightGBM, XGBoost, Gradient Boosting), Platt scaling either preserved the Brier score within ±0.005 or marginally improved it without changing the qualitative reliability pattern. Isotonic regression produced visually smoother calibration curves but introduced step artefacts at low predicted-probability ranges in two of the five models, suggesting overfitting to the calibration set; we therefore prefer Platt scaling for production deployment, with a recommendation to re-fit it on prospectively collected local data every twelve months.

subsections.title: Robustness of the XAI evidence to model resampling
subsections.content: Although the present paper takes the v2 CatBoost model as a frozen artefact, we additionally inspected the stability of the SHAP rankings against bootstrap resampling of the test set (n_bootstrap = 200). The top four global ranks (LH, Age, FSH, Testosterone) were preserved in 100% of bootstrap replicates. Ranks 5–8 (Body Weight, Sakamoto LT mL, BMI, Height) interchanged occasionally but never moved outside the top-eight set. The bilateral testicular-volume measures (RT XYZ Sono, Testicular Volume LT/RT clinical estimates, LT XYZ Sono) occupied ranks 9–13 with stable membership. Features with mean |SHAP| below 0.05 (E2, Sakamoto RT mL, Prolactin) showed greater rank instability but their contributions remained uniformly small. This bootstrap robustness check supports the claim that the multi-method XAI narrative is not artefactual to a particular test-set sampling.

subsections.title: Heterogeneity of local explanations across the predicted-probability spectrum
subsections.content: Inspecting the local SHAP explanations alongside the predicted probability for the five representative cases revealed substantive heterogeneity in the reasoning used by the model at different points of the probability spectrum. At low predicted probability (case 765, p̂=0.2836) the dominant contribution was demographic (Age) with a small set of counterbalancing favourable hormonal markers; at intermediate probability (case 2387, p̂=0.4299) the model used Seminal plasma pH as a strong negative contributor against a positive Age signal; at moderate-to-high probability (case 2162, p̂=0.7822) the model combined Age, FSH, and LH as positive contributors, exactly the canonical hypergonadotropic pattern of severe spermatogenic failure that paradoxically correlates with a higher chance of finding focal areas of preserved spermatogenesis at micro-TESE; at high probability (case 1833, p̂=0.8836) the model relied on the sonographic volume measurements rather than hormones; and at high probability (case 1814, p̂=0.7830) the model combined an unusual estradiol value with anthropometric and hormonal information. The clinical message is that the model does not uniformly weight features across patients but adapts its reasoning to the dominant signal available in each case — a behaviour that is invisible at the population level but is exposed by per-patient SHAP analysis.

subsections.title: CDSS feature mapping and integrated workflow
subsections.content: Combining the XAI evidence, the calibration analysis, and the DCA-derived clinically useful threshold window we operationalised an 11-feature CDSS (Table 4). Hormonal inputs (FSH, LH, Testosterone, E2, T/E2 ratio, Inhibin B) carry the largest weight, consistent with the SHAP ranking. Bilateral testicular volume is retained as a complementary anatomical signal. Age, BMI (derived from height and weight) and Karyotype/Klinefelter status are retained as demographic and genetic context. Partner_Age was excluded as biologically irrelevant to male testicular sperm retrieval (its high SHAP contribution in early model iterations was a confound for treatment urgency rather than a causal driver of testicular spermatogenesis). Each feature is paired with a validation colour code (red / teal / orange) and a CDSS scoring function (Table 4). The patient-facing output is a calibrated probability gauge with colour bands tied to the DCA-derived clinically meaningful threshold range 0.20–0.40, an abbreviated bar chart of the patient's top four feature contributions, and structured counselling text (Figure 7).

discussion: This study demonstrates that a competitive preoperative ML model for micro-TESE outcome [1] can be rendered transparent, calibrated, and clinically usable through a coordinated package of multi-method explainability, calibration analysis, decision curve analysis, and a structured CDSS specification. Three findings deserve emphasis. First, the top four global predictors — LH (mean |SHAP| 0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250), and Testosterone (0.212) — were identical and stable across SHAP, LIME, and PDP analyses, providing a method-independent biological signal. Second, calibration was acceptable without recalibration for the tree-based ensembles (Brier 0.201–0.213), with only the Stacking Ensemble (Brier 0.226) showing material miscalibration that responded to Platt scaling. Third, decision curve analysis demonstrated meaningful net clinical benefit across the threshold window 0.05–0.60, comfortably enclosing the preoperative counselling range 0.20–0.40 within which informed patient choice is most consequential [10].

Our XAI findings are biologically coherent. Elevated LH and FSH together with subnormal testosterone are the canonical endocrine signature of hypergonadotropic hypogonadism in NOA; recent domain studies have specifically highlighted the predictive value of the testosterone-to-LH ratio [22] and the testosterone-to-FSH ratio [23] for sperm retrieval. Our model, fed individual hormone levels rather than the ratios, recapitulates this evidence through its dominant SHAP contributions and produces monotonically interpretable PDP shapes (Figure 3). The non-monotone Age dependence aligns with the intuitive clinical observation that very young patients tend to present with severe primary spermatogenic phenotypes while older patients accumulate age-related testicular involution. Anthropometric variables (Body Weight, BMI, Height) carry secondary but consistent weight, potentially reflecting unmeasured metabolic-axis influences on testicular function. Crucially, the structured Karyotype and pathology variables — although they contribute essentially zero global SHAP in the preoperative model — are re-introduced via the CDSS through structured input fields and dedicated scoring logic, reflecting our deliberate decision to separate preoperative prediction (where intra-operative pathology cannot be used) from postoperative counselling and the planning of subsequent procedures.

The exclusion of Partner_Age deserves emphasis because earlier iterations of our pipeline assigned it a deceptively large SHAP importance (0.301). Partner Age has no plausible biological mechanism affecting male testicular spermatogenesis and serves at best as a proxy for couple duration or treatment urgency, both of which depend on health-system pathways rather than testicular biology [1]. Its removal redistributed importance towards endocrine and anatomical predictors, sharpening the biological interpretation of the model and reducing the risk of unintended bias against older female partners. This case study illustrates a more general lesson: a model with high apparent performance may still be relying on biologically implausible features and only multi-method XAI coupled with critical domain review can detect and correct such patterns [4].

From a methodological perspective, the choice of multi-method XAI over any single explainer is deliberate. SHAP is the de facto standard because of its theoretical guarantees [2,17], but it can be misleading under strong feature correlation and its global summary obscures heterogeneous local effects. LIME [3] provides intuitive local linear weights but is sensitive to neighbourhood sampling. PDPs [4–7] capture marginal trends but average over the joint distribution and can distort under interactions; accumulated local effects offer one principled extension [6]. By reporting all three jointly and comparing their rank orderings (Table 3), we expose the agreement across methods as evidence of robustness, while differences between SHAP and LIME explanations for specific patients flag cases where local model behaviour is unusual and may warrant clinical scrutiny. Recent applications of joint SHAP/LIME analysis to chronic kidney disease [31], comorbid depression and stroke [30], cardiac thrombosis after TAVR [32], and stroke risk in atrial fibrillation [34] illustrate the growing acceptance of this approach in clinical prediction.

Calibration is the most under-reported dimension of clinical ML evaluation, despite its critical importance for shared decision-making [8,9]. A Brier score of 0.213 for CatBoost — well below the non-informative reference of 0.367 — indicates that the predicted probabilities carry substantive information and align reasonably well with empirical positive fractions across deciles. The mild over-confidence we observed in the top decile of CatBoost predictions is a recurring pattern for gradient-boosted classifiers and is easily flagged in the reliability diagram (Figure 5). For clinical deployment we therefore recommend a one-step recalibration on prospectively collected local data using Platt scaling [11] or, for non-monotone deviations, isotonic regression [12] — the computational cost is trivial and the calibration improvement is often substantial, especially when the model is deployed at a centre with a different baseline success rate.

Decision curve analysis is the natural bridge between probabilistic prediction and clinical action. By integrating sensitivity, specificity, and the relative weight of false positives and false negatives across the full threshold range, DCA answers the question that AUC and Brier cannot: would using this model produce more or less net benefit than the default of always operating or never operating? Our analysis showed positive net benefit relative to both reference strategies across pt = 0.05–0.60, with substantial benefit margins in the preoperative-counselling window 0.20–0.40 (e.g., CatBoost 0.144 versus treat-all 0.043 at pt = 0.30). In clinical terms, this means that even relatively risk-tolerant patients (who only accept micro-TESE if the predicted probability of retrieval exceeds 0.20–0.30) and relatively risk-averse patients (who set a higher bar near 0.40) will benefit from being counselled with the model's probability rather than defaulting to operate-always or operate-never strategies. The fact that the Stacking Ensemble underperformed in this clinically relevant window despite being competitive on AUC illustrates the practical value of DCA in selecting the right model for deployment.

The clinical decision support framework presented in Table 4 and Figure 7 is, to our knowledge, the first explicit CDSS specification for micro-TESE preoperative counselling derived from a multi-method XAI analysis. Three design choices reflect the realities of fertility clinics in lower- and middle-income settings. First, the architecture is stateless (PHP 8.0+, Bootstrap, Chart.js) and can be hosted on cPanel shared hosting without a database, eliminating data-protection liability that often blocks deployment in resource-constrained environments [29]. Second, every input field carries an explicit reference range and a colour-coded validation that supports both data entry and clinician interpretation, helping non-expert users to detect implausible values before the prediction is computed [28]. Third, the patient-facing output deliberately combines a calibrated probability gauge with the top four SHAP contributors and an explicit recommendation to discuss alternatives when the predicted probability falls below 0.20–0.40, ensuring that the model is used as an aid to shared decision-making rather than as an automated trigger for surgical action [29].

It is instructive to situate our findings within the wider micro-TESE prediction literature. Zeadna and colleagues developed an early gradient-boosting model on a 119-patient conventional-TESE cohort with AUC 0.807 [19]; Bachelot and colleagues benchmarked eight algorithms on a 201-patient cohort, with a random forest achieving AUC 0.900 on a small prospective test set of n=26 [20]; Kobayashi and colleagues reported an AutoML system with AUC 0.725 [21]; Liu and colleagues developed an integrative logistic-regression model on 217 patients with AUC 0.781; and Deng and colleagues obtained AUC 0.720 on a 200-patient cohort with conventional logistic regression. More recent nomogram studies have scaled to multicentre settings [24,26], and a 2025 scoping review summarises the state of the field [27]. The defining contribution of the present work is not a marginal AUC gain but rather the combination of (a) a substantially larger single-centre cohort (n=2,413), (b) a multi-method XAI framework with explicit cross-method concordance, (c) joint reporting of calibration and DCA, and (d) a deployable CDSS specification. The 2026 World Journal of Men's Health review [18] specifically identifies these four elements — sample size, interpretability concordance, calibration, and clinical translation — as the current methodological frontier in AI for NOA, and our paper addresses each of them systematically.

Beyond the technical contribution, the value of the multi-method XAI and CDSS framework lies in its potential to support clinician trust. A recent systematic review of trust in AI-based CDSS among healthcare workers identifies transparent explanation, demonstrated calibration, and demonstrable clinical utility as the three most consistently reported preconditions for adoption [29]. Each of these is directly addressed by our reporting: SHAP, LIME, and PDP analyses make the model's reasoning transparent at both population and patient levels; the Brier-score and reliability-diagram evidence quantifies probability calibration; and the DCA establishes clinical utility across the preoperative-counselling threshold window. The patient-facing CDSS card (Figure 7) deliberately couples these three threads: a calibrated probability with colour bands tied to the DCA range, a bar chart of the patient's top four SHAP contributors, and an explicit recommendation to discuss alternatives below the clinically meaningful threshold band. We believe that this transparent structure is more important for clinical adoption than a marginal AUC gain.

Several limitations should be acknowledged. The cohort is from a single tertiary referral institute in Iran; although the analytical sample of 2,413 patients is, to our knowledge, among the largest single-centre cohorts reported for ML-based micro-TESE prediction [18,19,20,21,24,26,27], external multicentre validation is essential before clinical adoption. The retrospective design carries the usual risks of selection and information bias; we have mitigated these through explicit pathology-parsing audits, bilateral consistency checks, and the exclusion of biologically implausible predictors. The structured pathology and Karyotype features that are clinically meaningful at counselling time do not contribute meaningfully to the global preoperative SHAP because the preoperative pipeline correctly excludes intra-operative information; reintroducing them through the CDSS scoring functions is the appropriate way to use them without leakage. Finally, although our Brier scores compare favourably to the non-informative reference and DCA showed positive net benefit, our calibration analysis was internal; prospective calibration on locally collected data will be required at each new deployment site [8].

Three priorities should guide future work. First, prospective external validation in independent multicentre cohorts is needed both to confirm discrimination and calibration at sites with different baseline retrieval rates and case mixes [25,26]. Second, the CDSS specification in Table 4 should be field-tested in real clinical workflows with structured outcome capture, including qualitative measurement of clinician trust and patient comprehension [29]. Third, the integration of NLP-derived structured pathology variables — prepared in our companion paper [1] — with the multi-method XAI framework opens the possibility of an extended post-procedure CDSS that supports decisions about second-attempt micro-TESE and IVF strategy. Coupling the resulting evidence with periodic recalibration (Platt scaling on rolling 12-month windows of new data) and transparent reporting of model updates is the natural extension of the present work.
conclusions: A multi-method explainability analysis of the CatBoost preoperative micro-TESE model (AUC 0.8306, 95% CI 0.823–0.845; Brier 0.213) in 2,413 NOA patients revealed a method-stable and biologically coherent set of predictors dominated by LH (mean |SHAP| 0.317), Age (0.270), FSH (0.250) and Testosterone (0.212). Probability calibration was acceptable for all top-five tree-based models (Brier 0.201–0.226) and could be sharpened with Platt scaling where needed, and decision curve analysis demonstrated positive net clinical benefit across the threshold range 0.05–0.60, comfortably enclosing the preoperative counselling window 0.20–0.40.

These findings are operationalised in an 11-feature clinical decision support framework with explicit reference ranges, colour-coded input validation, transparent scoring functions, and a stateless web-deliverable architecture. The framework is designed to embed in routine preoperative counselling without imposing the data-protection and infrastructure burdens that often block clinical translation in resource-constrained settings. Prospective multicentre validation, on-site calibration, and structured measurement of clinician trust and patient comprehension are the necessary next steps before clinical adoption.

More broadly, this paper illustrates a pragmatic path for translating high-performing but opaque ML classifiers into clinically credible decision-support artefacts in reproductive medicine. The combination of multi-method XAI, calibration assessment, decision curve analysis, and an explicit CDSS specification with reference ranges and scoring logic provides a reproducible template that can be applied to other surgical-decision problems in andrology and beyond. As prediction models proliferate in clinical practice, the principal challenge is no longer demonstrating discrimination but demonstrating that the model can be trusted, understood, and acted upon. The framework presented here is offered as a transparent contribution to that emerging methodological consensus.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S3: Detailed partial-dependence descriptions for the top features
subsections.content:
One-way partial-dependence plots were generated for the eight features with mean |SHAP| > 0.10 in the global ranking. The qualitative behaviour of each PDP is summarised below.

LH (range 0–40 mIU/mL): monotonically increasing failure-risk SHAP from approximately −0.40 at LH < 4 mIU/mL to +0.40 around LH = 12–20 mIU/mL, with a plateau beyond 25 mIU/mL. Clinically consistent with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.

Age (range 18–70 y): non-monotone, with the most favourable region between roughly 30–40 y (SHAP ≈ −0.15) and rising failure-risk SHAP at both extremes (≈0.15 at <25 y and ≈0.20 at >55 y). The PDP shape reflects the clinical heterogeneity of NOA at age extremes.

FSH (range 0–40 mIU/mL): monotonically increasing failure-risk SHAP from −0.30 at FSH < 8 to +0.45 above 25 mIU/mL, mirroring the LH PDP shape but with a slightly later inflection point.

Testosterone (range 0–10 ng/mL converted units): monotonically decreasing failure-risk SHAP up to approximately 4 ng/mL, with a plateau above 5 ng/mL. The shape is consistent with the testosterone-to-LH and testosterone-to-FSH ratio literature.

Body Weight (range 50–120 kg) and BMI (range 18–40): both mildly U-shaped, with the most favourable region near normal-weight and a mild increase in failure SHAP at both extremes.

Sakamoto LT mL and RT XYZ Sono: monotonically favourable with increasing volume, with a plateau above approximately 6–8 mL for Sakamoto LT and approximately 10,000 mm³ for RT XYZ Sono. The bilateral pattern is symmetric in clinical interpretation.

Seminal plasma pH (range 7.0–8.5): a threshold effect around pH ≈ 7.8 separates a relatively flat low-risk region (pH < 7.8) from a positively-sloped failure-risk region above. LIME analyses elevated pH to a top-five local contributor in several patients.

PDP curves do not capture interactions; where strong interactions were suspected (e.g., LH × Testosterone, plausibly underlying the T/LH ratio literature) we additionally inspected the local SHAP scatter plots in Figure 3.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S4: TRIPOD+AI item-by-item source mapping
subsections.content: This table cross-references the TRIPOD+AI checklist items reported in the main TRIPOD+AI checklist section against the specific manuscript section, table, or figure where each item is addressed. Items 1–3 are covered in Title, Abstract, and Introduction. Items 4–9 (data source, eligibility, outcome definition, predictors, sample size, missing data) are covered in Methods subsections 'Study design and data source' and 'Predictors and preprocessing' and Table 1. Items 10–13 (statistical methods, model, calibration, leakage control) are covered in Methods subsection 'Model development and reference to the companion paper' and Tables 2 and 5. Item 14 (explainability) is covered in Methods subsection 'Multi-method explainability', Results subsections on SHAP, LIME, PDP, and cross-method concordance, Tables 2 and 3, and Figures 2–4. Items 16–18 (performance, calibration, clinical utility) are covered in Results subsections on calibration and DCA and Tables 5–6 and Figures 5–6. Items 19–23 are covered in Discussion. Items 24–25 are covered in the present supplementary materials.

subsections.title: Supplementary Table S5: CatBoost v2 hyperparameters, preprocessing pipeline, and operating metrics
subsections.content: CatBoost v2 champion hyperparameters (from nested 5×5 RandomizedSearchCV, 50 iterations per outer fold, random_state=42): depth = 6; iterations = 657; l2_leaf_reg = 3.51; learning_rate = 0.00617; preprocess__num__scaler = RobustScaler(); pathology encoding = ordinal_detailed_plus_raw. Preprocessing pipeline order: (1) RobustScaler fit on training folds only; (2) categorical encoding (one-hot/label) for Race, Habits, Karyotype, AZF microdeletions, Occupational toxin exposure; (3) binary 0/1 flags for comorbidities and surgical history; (4) missingness indicator binary features; (5) kNN imputation on continuous predictors after standardisation scaffolding; (6) regex-based pathology percentage parsing with maturation-arrest hierarchy enforcement; (7) SMOTE applied only to training folds; (8) CatBoost training with the hyperparameters above. Test-set operating metrics: AUC = 0.8306 (95% CI 0.823–0.845); accuracy = 0.7693; sensitivity = 0.6784; specificity = 0.822; PPV = 0.6883; NPV = 0.8152; F1 = 0.6832; MCC = 0.5019; Brier = 0.2131. Youden-J optimal threshold range: 0.444–0.507. Full development context is reported in the companion methodology paper [1].

subsections.title: Supplementary Note S1: Code, data, and CDSS deployment availability
subsections.content:
Code availability: The training and analysis pipeline was implemented in Python 3.11 using scikit-learn 1.4, lightgbm 4.x, xgboost 2.x, catboost 1.2, shap 0.45, and lime 0.2. All preprocessing, cross-validation, model fitting, calibration, decision-curve, and XAI code is structured as a reproducible pipeline with fixed random seeds (random_state = 42). The full code base is available upon reasonable request from the corresponding author and will be released as an open repository at the time of publication of the companion methodology paper [1]; the present XAI/CDSS analysis layer will be linked from that release.

Data availability: The patient-level data are governed by the Royan Institute IRB and contain potentially identifying clinical information. De-identified summary statistics and the data needed to reproduce the SHAP global ranking, the reliability diagrams, and the decision curves are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request and following a data-use agreement compliant with Iranian and EU GDPR-equivalent privacy regulations.

CDSS deployment artefact: The CDSS specification in Table 4 is provided as a stateless PHP 8.0+ application using Bootstrap 5.3.3 and Chart.js 4.4.3 (both delivered via CDN). The deployment package contains the form definitions, JavaScript validation logic with the colour-coded threshold rules in Table 4, the PHP scoring functions (cdss_score_lower_better, cdss_score_higher_better, cdss_score_centered), and the patient-facing prediction card described in Figure 7. The CDSS does not store any patient data, does not require authentication, and is deliberately designed to run on cPanel shared hosting to maximise accessibility in resource-constrained settings.

Reporting: This study is reported in accordance with TRIPOD+AI [14]. Risk of bias was self-assessed using PROBAST and the PROBAST+AI extension [15,16]; full domain-by-domain judgements are reported in the PROBAST section of this manuscript. Funding and conflict-of-interest statements appear in the Acknowledgements section.
